ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists specifically for Yakult green tea. Mapped to the closest parent class 'green tea beverage' (FOODON_03301099). The Yakult probiotic component is not captured in this ontology node.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers tea-based beverages broadly. No specific code exists for Yakult green tea or probiotic-enriched tea drinks in the FoodEx2 hierarchy as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Yakult green tea or probiotic tea beverages. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular food composition codes at this specificity.

preparation_method: served chilled or at room temperature
main_ingredients: Yakult (fermented skimmed milk drink with Lactobacillus casei Shirota)
optional toppings (e.g. grass jelly, aloe vera)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Yakult green tea in Singapore is approximately 500 g (medium cup), providing around 240 kcal, 62.2 g carbohydrates, and 47.7 g sugar per serving.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Foodo Nutrient Database — Yakult Green Tea (cr_id D08010095)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, and iron sourced directly from HPB Foodo Singapore (cr_id D08010095, confidence 0.95). HPB Foodo is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. Saturated fat is set to 0.0 g/100 g as naturally zero given that total fat is 0.0 g/100 g per HPB; trans fat is likewise set to 0.0 g/100 g on the same basis. Cholesterol and vitamin C were reported only by a non-approved delivery platform source and are therefore set to -1 (data not available from an approved source) and documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; University of Sydney GI Database sugar-sweetened beverage entries
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Yakult green tea as a composite beverage in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 60 (medium) based on the dominant sugar content (9.54 g/100 g sucrose-equivalent) and the analogous GI of sugar-sweetened tea beverages in the University of Sydney GI Database (sucrose GI ~65; diluted sugar-sweetened beverages typically 55–65). The Yakult probiotic component and green tea polyphenols may modestly attenuate postprandial glucose response, supporting a conservative estimate of 60. Glycemic load per 500 g serving = (60 × 62.2 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 37.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage — sugar at 9.54 g/100 g exceeds the 5 g/100 g beverage sugar threshold; independently raises blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines regardless of very low sodium (1 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid this beverage in its standard sweetened form; request no added sugar or substitute with plain unsweetened green tea, as the SSB sugar load independently elevates cardiovascular and blood pressure risk independent of sodium content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage — 9.54 g sugar/100 g with negligible dietary fibre (0.03 g/100 g) causes rapid glucose absorption; classified as an SSB triggering the diabetes hard gate for unsuitability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid this beverage; the high sugar content with near-zero fibre produces rapid postprandial glucose excursions, and the liquid form accelerates absorption — request a sugar-free or unsweetened version if consuming green tea with Yakult.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High added sugar load (9.54 g/100 g) in a liquid vehicle promotes hypertriglyceridaemia and endothelial inflammation; total fat is 0.0 g/100 g and sodium is very low (1 mg/100 g), limiting direct lipid and pressure risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this sugar-sweetened beverage due to the added sugar contribution to hypertriglyceridaemia and systemic inflammation; the green tea polyphenols (EGCG) and Yakult probiotics offer modest cardioprotective benefit that is outweighed by the sugar load in the sweetened preparation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar at 9.54 g/100 g promotes pro-inflammatory mediators that outweigh the anti-inflammatory benefit of green tea polyphenols (EGCG) and Yakult live cultures in this diluted sweetened preparation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should request no added sugar when ordering; plain unsweetened green tea provides anti-inflammatory EGCG polyphenols without the pro-inflammatory sugar load that drives inflammatory cytokine production.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yakult Green Tea is a popular Singaporean specialty tea beverage consisting of freshly brewed green tea blended with Yakult, a Japanese fermented skimmed milk probiotic drink containing Lactobacillus casei Shirota, and sweetened with sugar syrup. It is commonly sold at bubble tea and specialty drink stalls across Singapore and is available in medium (approximately 500 g) and large (approximately 700 g) cup sizes, often with customisable toppings such as grass jelly, aloe vera, or nata de coco. The drink has a light, slightly tangy flavour from the Yakult component balanced by the mild astringency of green tea, with a sweet finish from the added sugar syrup. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 48 kcal, 12.44 g carbohydrates (of which 9.54 g is sugar), 0.01 g protein, and 0.0 g total fat. Dietary fibre is near-zero at 0.03 g/100 g and sodium is very low at 1 mg/100 g. The high sugar content classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB), making it unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or rheumatoid arthritis in its standard sweetened form. The green tea component contributes epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG) polyphenols with antioxidant and mild anti-inflammatory properties, and the Yakult component provides live probiotic cultures that support gut microbiome health; however, these benefits are substantially attenuated by the added sugar load. A medium serving (500 g) delivers approximately 240 kcal and 47.7 g sugar, representing a significant discretionary sugar intake. Consumers managing blood glucose or cardiovascular risk should request a no-sugar or reduced-sugar version.

confidence_reason: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced directly from HPB Foodo Singapore (cr_id D08010095, confidence 0.95), the primary approved source for Singapore food records. Saturated fat and trans fat set to 0.0 as naturally zero given total fat is 0.0 per HPB. Cholesterol and vitamin C set to -1 as their only reported source was a non-approved delivery platform. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database sugar-sweetened beverage data, clearly documented.
notes: fat_g is 0.0 g/100 g — confirmed zero per HPB Foodo (cr_id D08010095); this is naturally zero for this diluted aqueous tea and probiotic beverage. saturated_fat_g is 0.0 g/100 g — naturally zero given total fat is 0.0 g/100 g per HPB Foodo; no fat fraction can exceed the total fat value. trans_fat_g is 0.0 g/100 g — naturally zero given total fat is 0.0 g/100 g per HPB Foodo. protein_g is 0.01 g/100 g (trace level from Yakult skimmed milk component). cholesterol_mg and vitamin_c_mg set to -1: the only reported values for these nutrients came from a non-approved delivery platform source (GrabFood); no approved source (HPB Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA) reports these values for this specific composite beverage as of 2026. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for this composite Singapore specialty tea beverage are not available in HPB Foodo or any other approved source as of 2026. GI of 60 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Yakult green tea exists. Glycemic load per 500 g serving = (60 × 62.2 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 37.3. Two portions recorded in source data: 1 large beverage (700 g) and 1 medium-sized cup (500 g); the medium cup (500 g) is used as the canonical default serving for GL calculation. The input had two entries both marked is_default: true — only the medium cup (500 g) is treated as the canonical default here.

improvement_actions_taken: Removed GrabFood as a nutrient source entirely — GrabFood is a forbidden delivery platform source.
nutrient_source now cites HPB Foodo only as the approved primary source.
cholesterol_mg and vitamin_c_mg changed from GrabFood-reported values (0.4 and 1.0) to -1 since no approved source reports these values for this food.
saturated_fat_g changed from 0.04 (GrabFood) to 0.0 — naturally zero given total fat is 0.0 per HPB Foodo; documented in notes.
trans_fat_g retained as 0.0 — naturally zero given total fat is 0.0 per HPB Foodo; documented in notes.
gi_source revised to cite University of Sydney GI Database explicitly as the approved GI source for the analogous estimate, removing any implication of non-approved sources.
glycemic_load_per_serving corrected to 37.3 (consistent with 500 g serving and 62.2 g carbohydrate).
notes updated to document all -1 fields and all zero-macro justifications per g3 and g4 requirements.
